title: Subprefectures of Japan

infobox.value: | [Prefectures](/wiki/Prefectures_of_Japan "Prefectures of Japan")
| * Subprefectures

| * [Designated cities](/wiki/Designated_cities "Designated cities") * [Core cities](/wiki/Core_cities_of_Japan "Core cities of Japan") * [Special cities](/wiki/Special_cities_of_Japan "Special cities of Japan") * [Cities](/wiki/Cities_of_Japan "Cities of Japan") ([List](/wiki/List_of_cities_in_Japan "List of cities in Japan")) * [Special wards (Tokyo)](/wiki/Special_wards_of_Tokyo "Special wards of Tokyo") * [Towns](/wiki/List_of_towns_in_Japan "List of towns in Japan") * [Villages](/wiki/List_of_villages_in_Japan "List of villages in Japan")
| * [Wards](/wiki/Wards_of_Japan "Wards of Japan")

(支庁, *shichō*) are a [Japanese](/wiki/Japan "Japan") form of self-government which focuses on local issues below the prefectural level. It acts as part of the greater administration of the state and as part of a self-government system.[[1]](#cite_note-louisiana80-1)

has two subprefectures, [Ōshima](/wiki/%C5%8Cshima_Subprefecture_(Kagoshima) "Ōshima Subprefecture (Kagoshima)") and [Kumage](/wiki/Kumage_Subprefecture "Kumage Subprefecture"), located in [Amami](/wiki/Amami,_Kagoshima "Amami, Kagoshima") and [Nishinoomote](/wiki/Nishinoomote,_Kagoshima "Nishinoomote, Kagoshima") respectively. They cover the islands between Kagoshima and Okinawa.
contains a single subprefecture, [Nishiusuki](/wiki/Nishiusuki_District,_Miyazaki "Nishiusuki District, Miyazaki"), a remote mountain district in the northwest corner of the prefecture.

contains four subprefectures that provide administrative services to residents of [outlying islands](/wiki/Tokyo_Islands "Tokyo Islands") under the Tokyo Metropolitan Government. The four branch offices are located at [Hachijō](/wiki/Hachij%C5%8D_Subprefecture "Hachijō Subprefecture"),[[3]](#cite_note-3) [Miyake](/wiki/Miyake_Subprefecture "Miyake Subprefecture"),[[4]](#cite_note-4) [Ogasawara](/wiki/Ogasawara_Subprefecture "Ogasawara Subprefecture")[[5]](#cite_note-5) and [Ōshima](/wiki/%C5%8Cshima_Subprefecture_(Tokyo) "Ōshima Subprefecture (Tokyo)").[[6]](#cite_note-6)

contains one subprefecture governing the [Oki Islands](/wiki/Oki_Islands "Oki Islands"). This is the closest Japanese government office to [Liancourt Rocks](/wiki/Liancourt_Rocks "Liancourt Rocks"), a small island group held by [South Korea](/wiki/South_Korea "South Korea") but claimed by [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan").

is divided into four subprefectures, each of which is located in one of the four main urban areas of the prefecture ([Yamagata](/wiki/Yamagata,_Yamagata "Yamagata, Yamagata"), [Shinjo](/wiki/Shinjo,_Yamagata "Shinjo, Yamagata"), [Yonezawa](/wiki/Yonezawa,_Yamagata "Yonezawa, Yamagata") and [Shonai plains](/wiki/Mikawa,_Yamagata "Mikawa, Yamagata")).
was divided into five subprefectures until 2003, when the branch offices were renamed citizens' centers (県民センター, *kenmin-sentā*).

had three subprefectures that provide services to the outlying islands of [Tsushima](/wiki/Tsushima_Subprefecture "Tsushima Subprefecture"), [Iki](/wiki/Iki_Island "Iki Island") and [Gotō](/wiki/Got%C5%8D,_Nagasaki "Gotō, Nagasaki"). They were replaced by Regional Offices and then by District Offices.

had two subprefectures, [Miyako](/wiki/Miyako_Subprefecture "Miyako Subprefecture") and [Yaeyama](/wiki/Yaeyama_Subprefecture "Yaeyama Subprefecture"), located on the islands of [Miyakojima](/wiki/Miyakojima "Miyakojima") and [Ishigaki](/wiki/Ishigaki_Island "Ishigaki Island") respectively. These offices provided prefectural government services to the isolated archipelagos surrounding both islands. They were abolished in March 2009 and duties taken over by the governments of [Miyakojima City](/wiki/Miyakojima,_Okinawa "Miyakojima, Okinawa"), [Miyako District](/wiki/Miyako_District,_Okinawa "Miyako District, Okinawa"), [Ishigaki City](/wiki/Ishigaki,_Okinawa "Ishigaki, Okinawa"), and [Yaeyama District](/wiki/Yaeyama_District,_Okinawa "Yaeyama District, Okinawa").

of Taiwan, which were remote prefectures that were much less populated, once considered "sub-", or "lesser", prefectures, i.e., [Hōko](/wiki/H%C5%8Dko_Prefecture "Hōko Prefecture") (the Pescadores), [Karenkō](/wiki/Karenk%C5%8D_Prefecture "Karenkō Prefecture") (Hualien) and [Taitō](/wiki/Tait%C5%8D_Prefecture "Taitō Prefecture") (Taitung).[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8) The offshore Hōko was home to the last two remaining subprefectures named **shichō**: Makō (馬公支廳) and Mōan (望安支廳).
Favro, S. (2010). [*Island Sustainability,* p. 195](https://books.google.com/books?id=Kc5xrqAD9fYC&pg=PA195&dq=) citing Tokyo Metropolitan Government, *Outline of Hachijo Subprefecture,* 2009.
Favro, [p. 195](https://books.google.com/books?id=Kc5xrqAD9fYC&pg=PA195&dq=) citing Tokyo Metropolitan Government, *Outline of Miyake Subprefecture,* 2009.
Yong Hong, Seoung. (2009). [*Maritime Boundary Disputes, Settlement Processes, and the Law of the Sea,* p. 148](https://books.google.com/books?id=mebBeRGmPAYC&pg=PA148&dq=).
Favro, [p. 195](https://books.google.com/books?id=Kc5xrqAD9fYC&pg=PA195&dq=) citing Tokyo Metropolitan Government, *Outline of Oshima Subprefecture,* 2009.
Kratoska, Paul H. (2006). [*Asian Labor in the Wartime Japanese Empire,* p. 102](https://books.google.com/books?id=-zvDJV3s6tAC&pg=PA102&dq=).
Morris, Andrew. (2010). [*Colonial Project, National Game: A History of Baseball in Taiwan,* p. 17](https://books.google.com/books?id=jqgWVRqAJ9QC&pg=PA17&dq=).

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Administrative divisions

| * [Designated cities](/wiki/Designated_cities "Designated cities") * [Core cities](/wiki/Core_cities_of_Japan "Core cities of Japan") * [Special cities](/wiki/Special_cities_of_Japan "Special cities of Japan") * [Cities](/wiki/Cities_of_Japan "Cities of Japan") ([List](/wiki/List_of_cities_in_Japan "List of cities in Japan")) * [Special wards (Tokyo)](/wiki/Special_wards_of_Tokyo "Special wards of Tokyo") * [Towns](/wiki/List_of_towns_in_Japan "List of towns in Japan") * [Villages](/wiki/List_of_villages_in_Japan "List of villages in Japan") |

| * [v](/wiki/Template:Administrative_divisions_of_Japan "Template:Administrative divisions of Japan") * [t](/wiki/Template_talk:Administrative_divisions_of_Japan "Template talk:Administrative divisions of Japan") * [e](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Template:Administrative_divisions_of_Japan "Special:EditPage/Template:Administrative divisions of Japan") |

**Subprefecture of Japan** (支庁, *shichō*) are a [Japanese](/wiki/Japan "Japan") form of self-government which focuses on local issues below the prefectural level. It acts as part of the greater administration of the state and as part of a self-government system.[[1]](#cite_note-louisiana80-1)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Subprefectures_of_Japan&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

They were given a definite form in 1878 (*Meiji 11*).[[2]](#cite_note-louisiana81-2)

The Meiji government established the sub-prefecture (郡, *-gun*) as an administrative unit.[[1]](#cite_note-louisiana80-1)

In 1888 (*Meiji 21*), the sub-prefecture as a form of self-government was officially [recognized](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/recognized "wikt:recognized") as more general than civic corporations like [cities](/wiki/City "City"), [towns](/wiki/Town "Town") and [villages](/wiki/Village "Village").[[2]](#cite_note-louisiana81-2)

Certain [prefectures of Japan](/wiki/Prefectures_of_Japan "Prefectures of Japan") are now, or once were, divided into subprefectures. The subprefecture is the jurisdiction surrounding a "branch office" of the prefectural government. Normally, the area of a subprefecture consists of a few to a dozen cities, towns, and/or villages. Subprefectures are formed to provide services of the prefectural government in geographically remote areas. They are usually not used in postal addresses.

## Existing subprefectures

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Subprefectures_of_Japan&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Existing subprefectures")]

* **[Hokkaidō](/wiki/Hokkaid%C5%8D "Hokkaidō")**, the largest prefecture by area in Japan, was divided into fourteen subprefectures. These were formed in 1897. The subprefectures did not include major cities, such as [Sapporo](/wiki/Sapporo "Sapporo") and [Hakodate](/wiki/Hakodate "Hakodate"), until 1922. In 2010 they were replaced by 9 General Subprefectural Bureaus and 5 Subprefectural Bureaus. *See: [Subprefectures in Hokkaidō](/wiki/Subprefectures_in_Hokkaid%C5%8D "Subprefectures in Hokkaidō")*
* **[Kagoshima](/wiki/Kagoshima_Prefecture "Kagoshima Prefecture")** has two subprefectures, [Ōshima](/wiki/%C5%8Cshima_Subprefecture_(Kagoshima) "Ōshima Subprefecture (Kagoshima)") and [Kumage](/wiki/Kumage_Subprefecture "Kumage Subprefecture"), located in [Amami](/wiki/Amami,_Kagoshima "Amami, Kagoshima") and [Nishinoomote](/wiki/Nishinoomote,_Kagoshima "Nishinoomote, Kagoshima") respectively. They cover the islands between Kagoshima and Okinawa.
* **[Miyazaki](/wiki/Miyazaki_Prefecture "Miyazaki Prefecture")** contains a single subprefecture, [Nishiusuki](/wiki/Nishiusuki_District,_Miyazaki "Nishiusuki District, Miyazaki"), a remote mountain district in the northwest corner of the prefecture.
* **[Tokyo](/wiki/Tokyo "Tokyo")** contains four subprefectures that provide administrative services to residents of [outlying islands](/wiki/Tokyo_Islands "Tokyo Islands") under the Tokyo Metropolitan Government. The four branch offices are located at [Hachijō](/wiki/Hachij%C5%8D_Subprefecture "Hachijō Subprefecture"),[[3]](#cite_note-3) [Miyake](/wiki/Miyake_Subprefecture "Miyake Subprefecture"),[[4]](#cite_note-4) [Ogasawara](/wiki/Ogasawara_Subprefecture "Ogasawara Subprefecture")[[5]](#cite_note-5) and [Ōshima](/wiki/%C5%8Cshima_Subprefecture_(Tokyo) "Ōshima Subprefecture (Tokyo)").[[6]](#cite_note-6)
* **[Shimane](/wiki/Shimane_Prefecture "Shimane Prefecture")** contains one subprefecture governing the [Oki Islands](/wiki/Oki_Islands "Oki Islands"). This is the closest Japanese government office to [Liancourt Rocks](/wiki/Liancourt_Rocks "Liancourt Rocks"), a small island group held by [South Korea](/wiki/South_Korea "South Korea") but claimed by [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan").
* **[Yamagata](/wiki/Yamagata_Prefecture "Yamagata Prefecture")** is divided into four subprefectures, each of which is located in one of the four main urban areas of the prefecture ([Yamagata](/wiki/Yamagata,_Yamagata "Yamagata, Yamagata"), [Shinjo](/wiki/Shinjo,_Yamagata "Shinjo, Yamagata"), [Yonezawa](/wiki/Yonezawa,_Yamagata "Yonezawa, Yamagata") and [Shonai plains](/wiki/Mikawa,_Yamagata "Mikawa, Yamagata")).

## Historical subprefectures

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Subprefectures_of_Japan&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Historical subprefectures")]

* **[Hyōgo](/wiki/Hy%C5%8Dgo_Prefecture "Hyōgo Prefecture")**, another geographically large prefecture, was divided into ten subprefectures, but these are now known as citizen's bureaus (県民局, *kenmin-kyoku*).
* **[Chiba](/wiki/Chiba_Prefecture "Chiba Prefecture")** was divided into five subprefectures until 2003, when the branch offices were renamed citizens' centers (県民センター, *kenmin-sentā*).
* **[Nagasaki](/wiki/Nagasaki_Prefecture "Nagasaki Prefecture")** had three subprefectures that provide services to the outlying islands of [Tsushima](/wiki/Tsushima_Subprefecture "Tsushima Subprefecture"), [Iki](/wiki/Iki_Island "Iki Island") and [Gotō](/wiki/Got%C5%8D,_Nagasaki "Gotō, Nagasaki"). They were replaced by Regional Offices and then by District Offices.
* **[Okinawa](/wiki/Okinawa_Prefecture "Okinawa Prefecture")** had two subprefectures, [Miyako](/wiki/Miyako_Subprefecture "Miyako Subprefecture") and [Yaeyama](/wiki/Yaeyama_Subprefecture "Yaeyama Subprefecture"), located on the islands of [Miyakojima](/wiki/Miyakojima "Miyakojima") and [Ishigaki](/wiki/Ishigaki_Island "Ishigaki Island") respectively. These offices provided prefectural government services to the isolated archipelagos surrounding both islands. They were abolished in March 2009 and duties taken over by the governments of [Miyakojima City](/wiki/Miyakojima,_Okinawa "Miyakojima, Okinawa"), [Miyako District](/wiki/Miyako_District,_Okinawa "Miyako District, Okinawa"), [Ishigaki City](/wiki/Ishigaki,_Okinawa "Ishigaki, Okinawa"), and [Yaeyama District](/wiki/Yaeyama_District,_Okinawa "Yaeyama District, Okinawa").

In addition, in 1907 Japan formed [Karafuto Prefecture](/wiki/Karafuto_Prefecture "Karafuto Prefecture") to govern the island of [Sakhalin](/wiki/Sakhalin "Sakhalin"). Karafuto was divided into four subprefectures: Toyohara (in present-day [Yuzhno-Sakhalinsk](/wiki/Yuzhno-Sakhalinsk "Yuzhno-Sakhalinsk")), Maoka (in present-day [Kholmsk](/wiki/Kholmsk "Kholmsk")), Esutoru (in present-day [Uglegorsk](/wiki/Uglegorsk,_Sakhalin_Oblast "Uglegorsk, Sakhalin Oblast")) and Shikuka (in present-day [Makarov](/wiki/Makarov,_Russia "Makarov, Russia")).

A number of islands gained by Japan in the [Treaty of Versailles](/wiki/Treaty_of_Versailles "Treaty of Versailles") were placed under the direction of a [South Pacific Prefecture](/wiki/South_Pacific_Mandate "South Pacific Mandate") (南洋庁, *Nan'yōchō*) from 1922 to 1945. This was divided into six subprefectures, on the islands of [Saipan](/wiki/Saipan "Saipan"), [Yap](/wiki/Yap "Yap"), [Palau](/wiki/Palau "Palau"), [Truk](/wiki/Chuuk_Lagoon "Chuuk Lagoon"), [Pohnpei](/wiki/Pohnpei "Pohnpei") and [Jaluit](/wiki/Jaluit "Jaluit"). In November 1943, the six subprefectures were merged into "eastern", "western" and "northern" subprefectures, which remained in place until the [Surrender of Japan](/wiki/Surrender_of_Japan "Surrender of Japan") in 1945.

### Taiwan

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Subprefectures_of_Japan&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Taiwan")]

See also: [Political divisions of Taiwan (1895–1945)](/wiki/Political_divisions_of_Taiwan_(1895%E2%80%931945) "Political divisions of Taiwan (1895–1945)")

[Taiwan during Japanese rule](/wiki/Taiwan_during_Japanese_rule "Taiwan during Japanese rule") initially had its prefectures – *ken* (県), later termed *shū* (州) and *chō* (庁) – subdivided into **shichō**. Most of the later subprefectures were named *gun* (郡; also "districts").
Some English texts translate "sub-prefecture" differently, using it instead for the **chō** of Taiwan, which were remote prefectures that were much less populated, once considered "sub-", or "lesser", prefectures, i.e., [Hōko](/wiki/H%C5%8Dko_Prefecture "Hōko Prefecture") (the Pescadores), [Karenkō](/wiki/Karenk%C5%8D_Prefecture "Karenkō Prefecture") (Hualien) and [Taitō](/wiki/Tait%C5%8D_Prefecture "Taitō Prefecture") (Taitung).[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8) The offshore Hōko was home to the last two remaining subprefectures named **shichō**: Makō (馬公支廳) and Mōan (望安支廳).
